title: What is a Grouped Convolution?
concept: A grouped convolution splits the input channels into separate groups, then convolves each group independently with its own set of filters — no group ever sees or mixes with another group's channels.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In a depthwise separable convolution, what does the depthwise step do to each input channel?

options.text: Applies one filter to that channel alone, with zero mixing across channels
Blends every channel together into a single output map
Deletes half of the input channels before the pointwise step runs

exercises.explain: Right — the depthwise step handles each channel in total isolation. Today: a middle ground between total isolation and total mixing.

cards.title: All or nothing?
cards.body: So far, convolution has been all-or-nothing on channels. A standard filter reaches across every input channel at once, mixing all of them into one output. What if you only wanted some of that mixing — not all of it, and not none of it either?

cards.title: What is a grouped convolution?
cards.body: A grouped convolution splits the input channels into G equal-sized groups. Each group gets its own separate set of filters, and those filters only ever look at their own group's channels — never any other group's. The outputs from all groups are stacked back together at the end.

cards.title: Separate production lines, one shipping dock
cards.body: Imagine a factory floor split into a few independent assembly lines, each with its own parts bin and its own workers. Line 1 never touches Line 2's parts, and vice versa. At the very end, every line's finished boxes get stacked onto the same shipping dock — that's the grouped conv's output.

cards.title: 4 channels, 2 groups
cards.body: Say an input has 4 channels: [A, B, C, D]. Split into 2 groups: Group 1 gets channels [A, B], Group 2 gets channels [C, D]. Group 1's filters only ever compute from A and B — they never see C or D. Group 2's filters only ever compute from C and D — they never see A or B.

cards.title: It's not two copies of the same filter
cards.body: It's tempting to picture grouped convs as just running the same filter twice on different data. In reality, each group learns its own independent filters — Group 1's filters can learn completely different patterns than Group 2's, because they never see the other group's channels to compare against.

cards.title: Standard vs. grouped: who sees what
cards.body: Same total channels, very different wiring:

left.text:
Every filter reaches
across all channels
Everything mixes

right.text:
Each filter reaches only
its own group's channels
Groups stay separate

exercises.prompt: In a grouped convolution with 2 groups, can a filter in Group 1 use information from Group 2's channels?

options.text: No — Group 1's filters only ever compute from Group 1's own channels
Yes, every filter always sees every channel regardless of grouping
Only during the very first training step

exercises.explain: Correct — that's the entire point of grouping: each group's filters are walled off from every other group's channels.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In a grouped convolution, the outputs from every group are eventually combined back into one stacked output.
exercises.explain: True — while the groups compute independently, their output channels are stacked together at the end to form the layer's full output.

exercises.sentence: A grouped convolution splits input channels into separate ___, each with its own independent filters.
exercises.explain: Right — "groups" is the key word; each group is handled by its own filter set, isolated from the rest.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes a grouped convolution?

options.text: Input channels are split into groups, and each group is convolved independently by its own filters
Every filter always convolves across the entire set of input channels, with no exceptions
Groups are created by splitting the image spatially into left and right halves

exercises.explain: Exactly — the split is by channel, not by spatial position, and each group's filters stay independent.

exercises.prompt: An input has 8 channels, split into 4 groups for a grouped convolution. How many of the 8 channels can a single filter in Group 1 ever compute from?

options.text: 2 — only Group 1's own channels (8 channels / 4 groups)
8 — every filter always sees the full channel depth

exercises.explain: Correct: 8 channels split evenly across 4 groups gives 2 channels per group, and a filter in Group 1 only ever reaches those 2. Next: an even bigger example — 64 channels split into groups.
